item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, Neil Gaiman's great. Thanks for the nice comment! :D I'm glad you like the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like something Neil Gaiman would write if he was in a good mood. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Result: Apple replaced with 76 larger than average apple seeds inside of a small tart tin.

Hahaha, that's totally what the faeries would do.

the potential for a breach of confidential data is ridiculously high, especially if someone tells the apple something REALLY juicy about the Foundation.

I actually didn't think of it this way! My main thought regarding the Keter class was that the Foundation has no idea where the tree will pop up next, and they don't really have any way of stopping the faeries from running their ripoff bakery anyway.

Great job!

Thanks! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. King: "My last psych eval shows that I probably should have retired a few years ago, but my counselor kept it quiet."
Result: Apple replaced with 76 larger than average apple seeds inside of a small tart tin.

I love how this is Keter. It initially seems like it really should be Euclid but the potential for a breach of confidential data is ridiculously high, especially if someone tells the apple something REALLY juicy about the Foundation.

Great job!

item.title: Re: Very good.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the nice comment and the upvote!

And yeah, I was definitely pretty hungry when I wrote this. That's what happens when you look through Wikipedia archives full of food pictures, I guess. d:

item.title: Very good.
item.content:encoded:
Has some amazing description, and certainly caught my Interest. Needless to say, upvote.

Now if you'll excuse me, another thing this SCP made me; is hungry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know after years of reading fairy tales as a kid I've never heard of fairies taking you prisoner. That's extremely amusing xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the nice comment!

And yeah, I remember when Keter meant the object had to also present some sort of imminent threat as well. I think that was changed several years back, so the "object class is based on difficulty of containment" guideline was clearer.

I also didn't really grow up with fairy tales, beyond the superstitions about numbers and certain actions around the house my (Chinese) parents told me about. I made up for this by raiding the school library's mythology and folklore books.

I'm glad you liked this, and I really appreciate the upvote! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is the first SCP of yours, I've checked out Zyn. Really fun and interesting idea, with a whimsical tone to it. As someone who didn't necessarily grow up with fairy tales, but knew a lot about them growing up, I understood a lot of what was being described, in particular the mushroom circles.

I also agree with the Keter classification, though I'm beginning to think we might need a new classification for Dangerous and Hard to contain. Since even I initially thought Keter meant 'dangerous' by default, when I first joined the site. That or we keep Keter as Dangerous and Hard to contain and make a new classification for hard to contain. ^^;

Anyway this got my +1 Upvote, it's really well done.

item.title: Re: Sweet Faeries
item.content:encoded:
Probably some other researcher at the Foundation Site the guy radioed before interacting with the anomaly.

Thank for upsnort I appreciate

item.title: Sweet Faeries
item.content:encoded:
What prompted the researcher to tell secrets to the apples?

Upsnort. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be my new favorite Zyn article.

:D

For future crosslink Wiki-Walk suggestions

I will definitely look into those, thanks!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be my new favorite Zyn article.

For future crosslink Wiki-Walk suggestions:

  • My SCP-2323 also has fairy circles and invisible fairies.
  • Drewbear's SCP-1323 and my SCP-2523 both involve esoteric exchanges with faeries that imply that faerieland has a different concept of economic value than humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep!! The more embarrassing and hard to surrender, the more valuable. But once a secret is told enough times, it's not a secret anymore… so you need more new secrets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of them, yeah. I would love to un-learn it for a day just to hear how weird it sounds to someone who doesn't speak it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But all Dutch words sound kinda funny though. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just write really, really slowly. The conceptualization phase often takes me weeks. I think about the idea a lot, and only write when I have the energy/inspiration.

The Dutch word for fairy tale is ''sprookje'' (with an o-sound like ''spoke'').

That is a cool fact to know!

Thanks for the upsnoot, am appreciate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah, they mainly use Neopoints there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get the feeling that somewhere in Fairyland, these secrets are being used as currency of some kind. Loved this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Devious Fairies

More like mischievous, but ripping people off all over the world is pretty devious d:

Weird mushroom circles?

They're said to be magical IRL! I read somewhere that if you step into one with both feet, the fairies will take you prisoner. If you step in with one foot, you can watch the fairies dance. But if you stay too long, then once you step out you'll notice that years and years have gone by in the outside world without you.

Fairy tales have oddly-specific mechanics.

I'm glad you enjoyed the idea! Thanks for the upvote. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whenever I think that mod stuff and the constant pruning of 914 makes me have little time to actually write, Zyn continues to post fantastic SCPs despite doing a ton more work for the site as well.

Upsnoot. The Dutch word for fairy tale is ''sprookje'' (with an o-sound like ''spoke''). That doesn't have to do with anything I previously said but it's a funny word.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fairy tales have a lot of seemingly-random themes!

Also, I didnt even know what a Manchester tart was until I looked up a list of English desserts in Wikipedia.

Thanks for the upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Devious Fairies
Embarrassing and humorous secrets
Weird mushroom circles?
Being scammed for a lackluster pastry

Funny and original idea that I enjoyed

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank much for updoot

Am oblige

Thank

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's Keter because I get the feeling the Foundation's really early in their investigation. They don't understand it, they don't know how to keep it from happening, they're working their butts off trying to pass it off as something mundane.

Yep! I wanted to write a Keter that wasn't going to end the world, but still left the Foundation out of its depth.

But you never see any faeries, and that was unexpected.

My take is that the fairies are on a strict timetable. Places to go, secrets to collect, sweets to sell. I'd always wondered why there were time limits in fairy tales like Cinderella, so this reflects those themes, a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
spoilers!
hide

I don't really understand why it has a keter rating. It gets created randomly but other than that containment seems fairly easy and the anomaly itself doesn't even pose a great threat.

Object class is based on difficulty of containment, not threat level. The Foundation has no way of preventing the manifestations or predicting them that reliably (slight spoilers: the fairies will show up wherever people are familiar with their fairy tales, so they pop up all over the world). Effectively, the manifestations are nigh-uncontained.

Also I was kinda hoping to know what happened exactly if you ate the apple.

During the selling time/Witching hour, you'd probably fall asleep because the fairies would be miffed you didn't "pay" for the apple. After they move the tree/bakery, they don't really care and it's the same as eating a regular apple.

The Foundation doesn't know all this though, because they didn't yet collect enough data to draw solid conclusions about eating the apples.

Thanks for the comment! I'm glad you liked the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea and an even better execution, the only thing I dislike about this article is that I don't really understand why it has a keter rating. It gets created randomly but other than that containment seems fairly easy and the anomaly itself doesn't even pose a great threat. Also I was kinda hoping to know what happened exactly if you ate the apple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ke-Keter?
Apple trees?
Mushrooms?
Tarts?

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I open the article and immediately see "Keter", "mushroom ring" and "Manchester tart" and that is how you hook.

I love how understated this is. It's Keter because I get the feeling the Foundation's really early in their investigation. They don't understand it, they don't know how to keep it from happening, they're working their butts off trying to pass it off as something mundane. But you never see any faeries, and that was unexpected. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Butterflies for the butterfly queen

Upvotes for the upvote god

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's clear that the faeries are interested in specific types of secrets, but I'm not exactly sure I'm seeing the common thread there.

Spoilers?
hide

The fairies want embarrassing secrets. They're the mischievous kind that likes gossip and playing pranks on humans.

Thanks again for reading this in chat! I really appreciate your patience with explanations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You always have such a fast turn around time with these.

Anyway, this is wonderfully whimsical in the style you have certainly come to be known for Zyn. It's clear that the faeries are interested in specific types of secrets, but I'm not exactly sure I'm seeing the common thread there.

Either way, a solid article with an amusing test log. +1.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
First off… thank you so much to everyone in chat who read through this before it went live!

  • XilasCrowe for reading and the mental image of "a bunch of heavily arms really buff MTF members sitting down and drawing crayon picture of faries"
  • Flashbulb for reading
  • weryllium for specific wording fixes
  • Rimple for technical fixes
  • Athenodora for specifics
  • Conwell for reading
  • CaptainKirby for phrasing and logistics

The images are from Wikimedia commons:

Fairy Circle on a suburban lawn. Forest Lake, south of Brisbane in Queensland (Public Domain)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fairy_ring_on_a_suburban_lawn_100_1851.jpg

Manchester tart (Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 3.0 Unported license) https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Manchester_tart.jpg

With regards to the SCP:

Potential spoilers!
hide
  • The apple tree is a fairy/faerie bakery.
  • The fairies expect humans to pay for their tasty treats in secrets.
  • Not just any secrets! Embarrassing secrets.
  • And it's not universally-embarrassing stuff either, it's stuff that the buyer feels is embarrassing. Researcher Harris is an intellectual and concerned more about being perceived as intelligent, moreso than his personal grooming.
  • The fairies definitely cheat people a bit so far as price for product goes. They're mischievous like that.
  • The Foundation has no clue how to approach this because a. they don't have enough data on manifestations to figure out more solid responses and b. if you screen people based on their secrets, those secrets aren't secrets anymore.

Hope you enjoyed! I was trying to write a short Keter piece here, and the idea's been sitting in my head for weeks and weeks. Originally the idea came to me when I found an old picture I took in college, of an apple just sitting on a bench. No one was near it, the apple was just by itself there. I wondered what its story might be.
